mnemonics.title: Graph Products Memory Aid
mnemonics.content: C-T-S-L: Cartesian (uses □, changes one coord), Tensor (×, needs BOTH), Strong (⊠, needs EITHER), Lexicographic ([ ], prioritize first). Remember: 'Cartesian puts you in a Grid, Tensor needs both, Strong takes either, Lexico leads with first'.

mnemonics.title: Complement Concept
mnemonics.content: Complement flips adjacency: friends become strangers, strangers become friends. Think of a party where non-acquaintances meet - original non-edges become edges.

mnemonics.title: Join Operation
mnemonics.content: Join = Connect Everything. The '+' sign in G+H means 'add all possible connections between the two graphs'. Like merging two separate friend groups where everyone meets everyone.

mnemonics.title: Isomorphism Check
mnemonics.content: ISO-FUND: Isomorphic graphs have Same degree sequence, Same component count, Same cycle lengths, Same connectivity, Same spectrum of eigenvalues. Not sufficient alone but necessary checks.

acronyms.expansion: Cartesian Product - changes one coordinate at a time
acronyms.usage: Creating grid graphs, parallel computation networks

acronyms.expansion: Tensor/Categorical/Kronecker Product - requires edges in BOTH graphs
acronyms.usage: Network reliability, graph theory proofs, bipartite graph products

acronyms.expansion: Strong Product - Cartesian OR Tensor adjacency
Lexicographic/Composition Product - first graph takes priority
acronyms.usage: Graph operations, creating complex graphs from simple ones

acronyms.expansion: Join - connects all vertices between graphs
acronyms.usage: Creating connected graphs, wheel graphs, complete bipartite graphs

visualTips.visualization: Imagine a chessboard: moving either horizontally OR vertically (not diagonally). Each move changes only one coordinate.
Think of a multiplication table where both conditions must be TRUE for adjacency. Like a logical AND operation.
Imagine chessboard with diagonal moves allowed - you can change one coordinate OR both (like both Cartesian and Tensor combined).
Picture two separate islands and build bridges connecting every vertex of island1 to every vertex of island2.
Flip a graph: existing edges become gaps, gaps become edges. Like converting a friendship network to 'who doesn't know whom'.

analogies.analogy: Combining two separate document files into one - you get all pages from both.
Finding common friends between two social circles - only those who belong to both groups.
Marrying two families - every member of one family now knows every member of the other.
A city grid where you can only move along streets (one direction at a time), not diagonally.
A double-lock system that opens only when BOTH locks are unlocked simultaneously.
Two puzzles with same number and arrangement of pieces but different pictures - the structure is identical.
